title: Shah Ismail I founds the Safavid Empire
description: Ismail I defeats the Aq Qoyunlu at the Battle of Sharur and establishes the Safavid dynasty in Tabriz, declaring Twelver Shi'ism the state religion of Iran — a decision that reshapes the sectarian map of Islam to this day.
sources: Roger Savory, Iran Under the Safavids
outcome: Created the first major Shia state since the Fatimids, permanently dividing the Islamic world along Sunni-Shia lines

title: Portuguese seize Hormuz and enter the Persian Gulf
description: Afonso de Albuquerque's fleet captures the strategic island of Hormuz at the mouth of the Persian Gulf, beginning European penetration of Muslim-controlled Indian Ocean trade routes.
sources: K.N. Chaudhuri, Trade and Civilisation in the Indian Ocean
outcome: Disrupted centuries of Muslim maritime dominance in the Indian Ocean and inaugurated the colonial era in the Muslim world

title: Portuguese conquer Malacca from the Sultanate
description: Albuquerque captures the Muslim trading hub of Malacca, severing the key strait between the Indian Ocean and the South China Sea from Islamic control.
sources: M.C. Ricklefs, A History of Modern Indonesia
outcome: Shifted control of Southeast Asian spice trade to European powers and galvanised Muslim resistance in Aceh and the Malay world

title: Battle of Chaldiran — Ottomans defeat Safavids
description: Sultan Selim I defeats Shah Ismail I at Chaldiran in northwest Iran, using Ottoman artillery and Janissary firearms against Safavid cavalry. The battle establishes the Ottoman-Safavid frontier that would persist for centuries.

figures.name: Selim I
figures.role: Ottoman sultan; commanded the victorious army

sources: Roger Savory, Iran Under the Safavids
outcome: Confirmed Ottoman military supremacy, established the Sunni-Shia geopolitical divide across the Middle East

title: Ottomans conquer Mamluk Egypt — Caliphate transfers
description: Selim I defeats the Mamluks at the Battle of Ridaniya and conquers Egypt. The last Abbasid shadow caliph al-Mutawakkil III reportedly transfers the caliphate to the Ottoman sultan, and Selim receives the keys to Mecca and Medina.

figures.name: Selim I
figures.role: Ottoman sultan; conquered Egypt and assumed custodianship of the Two Holy Mosques

sources: Ibn Iyas, Bada'i al-Zuhur fi Waqa'i al-Duhur
outcome: Made the Ottomans the dominant Sunni power and custodians of Islam's holiest cities for four centuries

title: Suleiman the Magnificent ascends Ottoman throne
description: Suleiman I succeeds his father Selim I as Ottoman sultan. His 46-year reign will mark the zenith of Ottoman power, law, art and architecture.

figures.name: Suleiman the Magnificent
figures.role: Ottoman sultan; began the longest and most celebrated reign in Ottoman history

sources: Halil Inalcik, The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age
outcome: Inaugurated the golden age of Ottoman civilisation, law and territorial expansion

title: Battle of Panipat — Babur founds Mughal Empire
description: Zahir ud-Din Babur, a Timurid prince from Central Asia, defeats Sultan Ibrahim Lodi's numerically superior Delhi Sultanate forces at the First Battle of Panipat using gunpowder artillery — founding the Mughal dynasty.

figures.name: Babur
figures.role: founder of the Mughal Empire; commanded the invading Timurid army

outcome: Established Muslim Mughal rule over India that would last 332 years and produce some of the world's greatest architecture and culture

title: Battle of Mohacs — Ottomans conquer Hungary
description: Suleiman the Magnificent defeats King Louis II of Hungary at Mohacs, killing the king and opening Central Europe to Ottoman expansion.

figures.name: Suleiman the Magnificent
figures.role: Ottoman sultan; led the army that crushed Hungarian resistance

outcome: Extended Ottoman power deep into Central Europe and established Ottoman Hungary for 150 years

title: Ottoman siege of Vienna
description: Suleiman the Magnificent besieges Vienna but fails to take the city due to extended supply lines, early winter and determined Habsburg defence — marking the high-water mark of Ottoman expansion into Europe.

figures.name: Suleiman the Magnificent
figures.role: Ottoman sultan; commanded the besieging forces

outcome: Established the approximate western limit of Ottoman expansion into Europe

title: Ahmad Sirhindi born — future Mujaddid of second millennium
description: Ahmad Sirhindi is born in Sirhind, Punjab. He will become the most influential reformer of Indian Islam, challenging Emperor Akbar's religious syncretism and earning the title 'Mujaddid-e-Alf-e-Thani' (Renewer of the Second Millennium).

figures.name: Ahmad Sirhindi
figures.role: future Naqshbandi reformer and 'Renewer of the Second Millennium'

outcome: His reform movement restored orthodox Sunni practice in Mughal India and influenced Islamic revivalism globally

title: Mimar Sinan builds the Suleymaniye Mosque
description: The Ottoman architect Mimar Sinan completes the Suleymaniye Mosque in Istanbul, commissioned by Suleiman the Magnificent. The mosque complex includes a hospital, school, library and public kitchen — a masterpiece of Islamic architecture.

figures.name: Mimar Sinan
figures.role: chief Ottoman architect; designed the Suleymaniye complex

figures.name: Suleiman the Magnificent
figures.role: patron; commissioned the mosque as the centrepiece of Ottoman Istanbul

sources: Gulru Necipoglu, The Age of Sinan
outcome: Produced one of the greatest architectural works in Islamic history, rivalling the Hagia Sophia

title: Akbar becomes Mughal Emperor at age 13
description: After the death of Humayun, 13-year-old Jalal ud-Din Akbar ascends the Mughal throne under the regency of Bairam Khan. His reign will bring religious tolerance, administrative genius and a cultural golden age to India.

figures.name: Akbar the Great
figures.role: Mughal emperor; began the greatest period of Mughal civilisation

figures.name: Humayun
figures.role: father; Mughal emperor whose sudden death brought Akbar to the throne

outcome: Inaugurated the golden age of Mughal India — religious tolerance, administrative innovation and cultural brilliance

title: Shaykh Abd al-Wahhab al-Sha'rani writes al-Tabaqat al-Kubra
description: The Egyptian Sufi scholar and jurist al-Sha'rani completes his biographical dictionary of Sufi saints, preserving the spiritual heritage of Egyptian and Levantine mysticism.

figures.name: Abd al-Wahhab al-Sha'rani
figures.role: Sufi scholar; authored major biographical work on saints

outcome: Preserved the biographies and teachings of hundreds of Sufi masters for future generations

title: Ottoman siege of Malta fails
description: The Knights Hospitaller successfully defend Malta against a massive Ottoman naval expedition, halting Ottoman expansion in the western Mediterranean.
outcome: Marked the end of Ottoman naval dominance in the western Mediterranean

title: Battle of Lepanto — Christian fleet defeats Ottomans
description: A combined Christian fleet of the Holy League under Don John of Austria destroys the Ottoman fleet at Lepanto off western Greece — the last major galley battle in history.
outcome: Ended Ottoman naval dominance in the Mediterranean, though the Ottomans rebuilt their fleet within a year

title: Mimar Sinan completes the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne
description: At the age of 85, Mimar Sinan completes the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne, which he considers his masterpiece — surpassing even the dome span of the Hagia Sophia.

figures.name: Mimar Sinan
figures.role: chief Ottoman architect; considered this his greatest work

sources: Gulru Necipoglu, The Age of Sinan
outcome: Achieved the architectural ambition of surpassing the Hagia Sophia, considered Sinan's masterpiece

title: Islam spreads to Java, Moluccas and Borneo
description: Muslim merchants and Sufi missionaries complete the Islamisation of the major Indonesian islands. The Sultan of Ternate controls the spice islands and local rulers increasingly adopt Islam as their faith and legal framework.

figures.name: Hamza Fansuri
figures.role: Sufi poet and mystic; key figure in the spread of Islam in the Malay archipelago

sources: M.C. Ricklefs, A History of Modern Indonesia
outcome: Made Indonesia the world's most populous Muslim nation — a legacy that persists today

title: Shah Abbas I begins reign — Safavid golden age
description: Shah Abbas I assumes power in the Safavid Empire at age 17. He will reform the military, move the capital to Isfahan, and preside over the greatest era of Persian culture, art and architecture.
sources: Roger Savory, Iran Under the Safavids
outcome: Inaugurated the Safavid golden age — Isfahan became one of the world's greatest cities under his rule

title: Baha-ud-Din Naqshband's spiritual legacy dominates Central Asia
description: By the end of the 16th century, the Naqshbandi Sufi order founded by Baha-ud-Din Naqshband has become the dominant spiritual force across Central Asia, Ottoman Turkey and Mughal India, shaping Islamic governance and reform.

figures.name: Baha-ud-Din Naqshband
figures.role: founder of the Naqshbandi order whose legacy shaped Islamic reform

figures.name: Khwaja Baqi Billah
figures.role: Naqshbandi master who brought the order to Mughal India

outcome: The Naqshbandi order became the most politically influential Sufi movement in Islamic history

title: British East India Company founded
description: Queen Elizabeth I grants a royal charter to the East India Company, beginning the commercial penetration of Mughal India that will eventually lead to British colonial rule.
sources: K.N. Chaudhuri, The English East India Company
outcome: The beginning of European commercial dominance in the Indian Ocean that would ultimately displace Muslim power

title: Emperor Akbar dies — Jahangir succeeds
description: The death of Akbar the Great ends 49 years of transformative Mughal rule. His son Jahangir succeeds him, continuing the dynasty's artistic patronage though with less administrative innovation.

figures.name: Akbar the Great
figures.role: Mughal emperor; died after the most consequential reign in Indian history

figures.name: Jahangir I
figures.role: son and successor; continued Mughal artistic patronage

outcome: Transitioned Mughal India from its reformist age to its artistic golden age

title: Nur Jahan becomes Mughal Empress and de facto ruler
description: Mehr-un-Nissa marries Emperor Jahangir and takes the title Nur Jahan (Light of the World). She becomes the most powerful woman in Mughal history, issuing coins in her own name and making key political decisions.

figures.name: Nur Jahan
figures.role: empress; the most powerful woman in Mughal history

figures.name: Jahangir I
figures.role: husband; Mughal emperor who shared power with Nur Jahan

sources: Ruby Lal, Empress: The Astonishing Reign of Nur Jahan
outcome: Demonstrated that women could wield supreme political power in an Islamic empire

title: Ahmad Sirhindi's Maktubat transforms Indian Islam
description: The letters (Maktubat) of Ahmad Sirhindi circulate widely after his death in 1624, establishing the theological framework for orthodox Sunni revival in Mughal India against Akbar's syncretic Din-i-Ilahi.

figures.name: Ahmad Sirhindi
figures.role: Naqshbandi reformer; his posthumous letters reshaped Indian Sunni orthodoxy

outcome: Permanently restored orthodox Sunni practice in South Asian Islam and influenced global Islamic revivalism

title: Shah Jahan begins building the Taj Mahal
description: Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan commissions the construction of the Taj Mahal in Agra as a mausoleum for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died in childbirth. It will take 22 years and 20,000 workers to complete.

figures.name: Shah Jahan
figures.role: Mughal emperor; commissioned the Taj Mahal in memory of his wife

sources: Ebba Koch, The Complete Taj Mahal
outcome: Created the most famous monument of Islamic civilisation and one of the world's most recognisable buildings

title: Treaty of Zuhab ends Ottoman-Safavid Wars
description: The Ottoman and Safavid empires sign the Treaty of Zuhab (Qasr-e-Shirin), establishing a border between them that largely persists as the modern Iraq-Iran frontier. The Ottomans retain Iraq; the Safavids keep the Caucasus.
sources: Roger Savory, Iran Under the Safavids
outcome: Established the approximate modern border between Iraq and Iran, ending 150 years of Ottoman-Safavid warfare

title: Dara Shikoh writes Majma al-Bahrayn
description: Mughal prince Dara Shikoh completes Majma al-Bahrayn (The Mingling of the Two Oceans), comparing Islamic Sufi and Hindu Vedantic mystical concepts and arguing for their essential unity.

figures.name: Dara Shikoh
figures.role: Mughal prince; authored pioneering work on Islamic-Hindu mystical parallels

outcome: Produced one of the most daring interfaith theological works in Islamic history, influencing later religious dialogue

title: Aurangzeb deposes Shah Jahan and takes the Mughal throne
description: After a war of succession, the orthodox Aurangzeb defeats his brothers (including the Sufi-minded Dara Shikoh, whom he executes) and imprisons his father Shah Jahan in Agra Fort.

figures.name: Aurangzeb
figures.role: victor of the succession war; became 6th Mughal emperor

figures.name: Shah Jahan
figures.role: deposed father; imprisoned in Agra Fort with a view of the Taj Mahal

figures.name: Dara Shikoh
figures.role: defeated elder brother; executed for heresy

outcome: Shifted Mughal policy from religious tolerance toward orthodox Sunni enforcement, with lasting consequences for India

title: Mulla Muhsin Fayd Kashani completes al-Wafi
description: The Persian philosopher and hadith scholar Mulla Muhsin Fayd Kashani completes al-Wafi, a monumental encyclopaedia harmonising the four major Shia hadith collections.

figures.name: Mulla Muhsin Fayd Kashani
figures.role: Shia philosopher-scholar; synthesised the four canonical hadith books

sources: Todd Lawson, 'Akhbari Shi'i Approaches to Tafsir'
outcome: Created the most comprehensive systematisation of Shia hadith literature in Islamic history

title: Ottoman conquest of Crete completed after 25-year siege
description: After one of the longest sieges in history (1645-1669), the Ottomans finally capture the Venetian fortress of Candia (Heraklion) on Crete, completing their control of the eastern Mediterranean.
outcome: Completed Ottoman naval dominance of the eastern Mediterranean

title: Aurangzeb completes the Badshahi Mosque in Lahore
description: Emperor Aurangzeb completes the Badshahi Mosque in Lahore, the largest mosque in the world at the time, capable of accommodating 100,000 worshippers.

figures.name: Aurangzeb
figures.role: Mughal emperor; commissioned the Badshahi Mosque

sources: Catherine Asher, Architecture of Mughal India
outcome: Created the largest mosque of its era and an enduring symbol of Mughal Lahore

title: Second siege of Vienna — Ottoman defeat begins decline
description: Grand Vizier Kara Mustafa Pasha leads 150,000 Ottoman troops in a second siege of Vienna. The siege is broken by a combined Habsburg-Polish relief force under King Jan III Sobieski, marking the beginning of Ottoman territorial retreat in Europe.
sources: Andrew Wheatcroft, The Enemy at the Gate
outcome: Triggered the Ottoman retreat from Europe and signalled the shift in global power toward the West

title: Zeb-un-Nissa — Mughal princess and major poet
description: Princess Zeb-un-Nissa, daughter of Aurangzeb, compiles her Persian poetry collection. Despite being imprisoned by her father for supporting her brother's rebellion, she produces some of the finest Persian verse of the era.

figures.name: Zeb-un-Nissa
figures.role: Mughal princess and Persian poet; confined by Aurangzeb

figures.name: Aurangzeb
figures.role: her father; imprisoned her for political disloyalty

outcome: Demonstrated women's literary achievement at the highest levels of the Mughal court

title: Birth of Shah Waliullah and Ibn Abd al-Wahhab
description: In the same year, two of the most consequential Islamic reformers are born: Shah Waliullah in Delhi and Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab in Najd. Their reform movements — one integrative, one puritanical — will reshape the modern Muslim world.

figures.name: Shah Waliullah
figures.role: born in Delhi; would become India's greatest Islamic thinker

figures.name: Ibn Abd al-Wahhab
figures.role: born in Najd; would found the Wahhabi movement that allied with the House of Saud

sources: J.M.S. Baljon, Religion and Thought of Shah Wali Allah
outcome: Both would launch reform movements that fundamentally shaped modern Sunni Islam in divergent directions

title: Death of Aurangzeb — Mughal decline begins
description: The death of Emperor Aurangzeb at age 88 after a 49-year reign triggers the rapid disintegration of the Mughal Empire. Succession wars, regional revolts and rising Maratha power fragment the subcontinent.

figures.name: Aurangzeb
figures.role: 6th Mughal emperor; his death triggered imperial fragmentation

outcome: Ended Mughal centralised power and opened India to European colonialism

title: Afghan siege of Isfahan — Safavid Empire collapses
description: The Ghilzai Afghans under Mahmud Hotak besiege and capture Isfahan after a devastating seven-month siege that kills hundreds of thousands. Shah Sultan Husayn surrenders, ending 220 years of Safavid rule.
sources: Roger Savory, Iran Under the Safavids
outcome: Ended the Safavid dynasty and plunged Iran into decades of chaos before the rise of Nader Shah

title: Nader Shah sacks Delhi — loots the Peacock Throne
description: The Persian conqueror Nader Shah invades Mughal India, defeats the Mughal army at the Battle of Karnal, and sacks Delhi — carrying off the Peacock Throne, the Koh-i-Noor diamond and treasure worth an estimated £87.5 million.
sources: Michael Axworthy, The Sword of Persia: Nader Shah
outcome: Fatally weakened the Mughal Empire and demonstrated the power vacuum in the Muslim world

title: Ibn Abd al-Wahhab allies with Muhammad ibn Saud
description: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and Muhammad ibn Saud forge the Pact of Najd in Diriyah, creating a religio-political alliance: Ibn Saud provides military power, Ibn Abd al-Wahhab provides religious legitimacy.

figures.name: Ibn Abd al-Wahhab
figures.role: religious reformer; provided theological legitimacy for the Saudi state

sources: Alexei Vassiliev, The History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: Founded the Saudi-Wahhabi alliance that produced three Saudi states and ultimately modern Saudi Arabia

title: Shah Waliullah completes Hujjat Allah al-Baligha
description: The Delhi scholar Shah Waliullah Dehlavi completes his magnum opus Hujjat Allah al-Baligha (The Conclusive Argument from God), a comprehensive reinterpretation of Islamic law and theology that seeks to reconcile all four Sunni schools.

figures.name: Shah Waliullah
figures.role: scholar-reformer; produced the most important Islamic theological work of the 18th century

sources: J.M.S. Baljon, Religion and Thought of Shah Wali Allah
outcome: Produced the intellectual foundation for Islamic reform in South Asia that influenced every subsequent movement

title: Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai's Shah Jo Risalo established
description: The spiritual poetry collection Shah Jo Risalo by the Sindhi Sufi poet Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai becomes established as the supreme literary and spiritual work of Sindhi civilisation after his death in 1752.

figures.name: Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai
figures.role: Sindhi Sufi poet; author of the Shah Jo Risalo

sources: H.T. Sorley, Shah Abdul Latif of Bhit
outcome: Established the greatest work of Sindhi literature and a masterpiece of Sufi poetry

title: Ahmad al-Tijani founds the Tijaniyya Sufi order
description: Ahmad al-Tijani establishes the Tijaniyya order in North Africa, teaching a simplified path to spiritual realisation that will become one of the most widespread Sufi orders in West and North Africa.

figures.name: Ahmad al-Tijani
figures.role: founder of the Tijaniyya order

outcome: Created one of the most influential Sufi orders in Africa, with millions of followers to this day

title: Waris Shah writes Heer Ranjha
description: The Punjabi Sufi poet Waris Shah completes his retelling of the folk love story Heer Ranjha, transforming it into a profound Sufi allegory that becomes the greatest work of Punjabi literature.

figures.name: Waris Shah
figures.role: Sufi poet; author of the definitive Heer Ranjha

outcome: Created the supreme masterpiece of Punjabi Sufi literature, still recited across Punjab today

title: Treaty of Kucuk Kaynarca — Russia gains Muslim territory
description: The Ottoman Empire signs the Treaty of Kucuk Kaynarca with Russia, ceding Crimea and granting Russia the right to protect Orthodox Christians in Ottoman lands — the first major territorial loss to a Christian power.
outcome: Began the 'Eastern Question' that would dominate European diplomacy and Ottoman decline for 150 years

title: Napoleon invades Egypt — the modern era begins
description: Napoleon Bonaparte lands in Egypt with 36,000 troops and a team of scholars, defeating the Mamluk cavalry at the Battle of the Pyramids. The expedition shocks the Muslim world and introduces modern European science and institutions to the Middle East.
sources: Al-Jabarti, Ajaib al-Athar fi al-Tarajim wa al-Akhbar
outcome: Inaugurated the modern era for the Muslim world — demonstrating European technological supremacy and triggering reform movements

title: Saudis capture Mecca — Wahhabi movement ascendant
description: The First Saudi State under Abdulaziz ibn Muhammad captures Mecca and imposes Wahhabi reforms on the holy city, destroying ornate shrines and tombs they consider idolatrous.

figures.name: Ibn Abd al-Wahhab
figures.role: founder of the Wahhabi movement whose teachings motivated the conquest (died 1792)

sources: Alexei Vassiliev, The History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: Demonstrated the revolutionary potential of Islamic reform and provoked an Ottoman military response

title: Usman dan Fodio declares jihad — Sokoto Caliphate founded
description: The Fulani scholar Usman dan Fodio declares a jihad against the Hausa rulers of northern Nigeria, establishing the Sokoto Caliphate — the largest state in 19th-century Africa and the most successful Islamic reform movement of its era.

figures.name: Usman dan Fodio
figures.role: scholar-reformer; founded the Sokoto Caliphate through jihad

figures.name: Nana Asma'u
figures.role: his daughter; poet, educator and leader of women's education

figures.name: Muhammadu Bello
figures.role: his son and first Sultan of Sokoto

outcome: Created the largest state in 19th-century Africa and a model for Islamic governance and women's education

title: Mahmud II destroys the Janissaries — Ottoman modernisation begins
description: Ottoman Sultan Mahmud II massacres the Janissary corps in the 'Auspicious Incident', abolishing the once-elite military force that had become a reactionary obstacle to reform, and begins building a modern European-style army.
outcome: Removed the main obstacle to Ottoman military modernisation and cleared the way for the Tanzimat reforms

title: France invades Algeria — Islamic colonisation begins
description: France invades and conquers Algeria, beginning 132 years of colonial rule. Amir Abd al-Qadir al-Jaza'iri leads the resistance, becoming a symbol of Muslim anti-colonial struggle.

figures.name: Abd al-Qadir al-Jaza'iri
figures.role: Algerian amir and Sufi scholar; led resistance against French colonisation

sources: John Kiser, Commander of the Faithful
outcome: Began the European colonisation of the Arab-Muslim heartland and galvanised Islamic resistance movements globally

title: Tanzimat reforms begin in the Ottoman Empire
description: Sultan Abdulmecid I issues the Hatt-i Sherif of Gulhane, inaugurating the Tanzimat (Reorganisation) era of legal and administrative modernisation, including equal rights for non-Muslim subjects.
sources: Erik Jan Zurcher, Turkey: A Modern History
outcome: Launched the most ambitious modernisation programme in the Muslim world, inspiring reformers from Cairo to Delhi

title: British abolish Mughal Empire after Indian Rebellion
description: After the failed Indian Rebellion of 1857, the British formally depose the last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar and exile him to Rangoon, ending 332 years of Mughal rule and establishing direct British Crown rule over India.
outcome: Ended Islamic political sovereignty in India and traumatised the Muslim community, spurring reform movements

title: Deoband seminary founded — Islamic education reform
description: The Darul Uloom Deoband is founded in northern India to preserve Islamic learning and produce scholars after the fall of the Mughal Empire, becoming the most influential Islamic seminary in the Sunni world after Al-Azhar.
sources: Barbara Metcalf, Islamic Revival in British India
outcome: Created a new model of Islamic education that shaped religious thought across South and Central Asia

title: Suez Canal opens — reshapes Muslim trade routes
description: The Suez Canal is completed and opens for navigation, dramatically reducing the sea journey between Europe and Asia and making Egypt a critical strategic asset, ultimately leading to British occupation.
outcome: Transformed the geopolitics of the Muslim world by making Egypt a linchpin of global trade and European imperial strategy

title: Allama Iqbal born — future poet-philosopher of Islam
description: Muhammad Iqbal is born in Sialkot, Punjab. He will become the greatest Urdu and Persian poet of the 20th century and the intellectual father of Pakistan.

figures.name: Allama Iqbal
figures.role: born in Sialkot; would become the poet-philosopher of the Islamic revival

sources: Iqbal, The Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam
outcome: Would produce the philosophical vision for Islamic modernity and the intellectual foundation for Pakistan

title: Jamal al-Din al-Afghani expelled from Egypt
description: The pan-Islamist activist Jamal al-Din al-Afghani is expelled from Egypt by the British-backed Khedive after years of political agitation, teaching and newspaper publishing that galvanised the Islamic reform movement.

figures.name: Jamal al-Din al-Afghani
figures.role: pan-Islamist activist and reformer; expelled for political agitation

sources: Nikki Keddie, An Islamic Response to Imperialism
outcome: Spread pan-Islamic ideas that influenced Muhammad Abduh, Rashid Rida and every subsequent Islamic reform movement

title: Muhammad Ahmad declares himself the Mahdi in Sudan
description: Muhammad Ahmad ibn Abd Allah declares himself the Mahdi (guided one) in Sudan and launches a revolt against Egyptian-Ottoman and British rule, establishing a theocratic state.

location.modern: Aba Island, White Nile State, Sudan

sources: P.M. Holt, The Mahdist State in the Sudan
outcome: Created the only successful anti-colonial Islamic state of the 19th century, lasting until 1898

title: British military occupation of Egypt
description: Britain occupies Egypt militarily to protect the Suez Canal and suppress the Urabi revolt, beginning a 72-year occupation that transforms Egyptian politics and culture.
sources: M.W. Daly, The Cambridge History of Egypt
outcome: Brought the most important Arab country under European control, galvanising the Islamic and nationalist reform movements

title: Muhammad Abduh publishes reformist Quranic commentary
description: Muhammad Abduh, Grand Mufti of Egypt and student of al-Afghani, begins publishing his modernist Quranic exegesis and legal opinions that argue Islam is compatible with reason, science and modernity.

figures.name: Muhammad Abduh
figures.role: Grand Mufti of Egypt; pioneer of Islamic modernism

outcome: Founded the Islamic modernist school that shaped Muslim intellectual life for the next century

title: Mirza Ghalib's Urdu ghazals established as canon
description: By the end of the 19th century, the ghazals of Mirza Ghalib (died 1869) are established as the supreme achievement of Urdu poetry, studied and recited across the Muslim world from Istanbul to Dhaka.

figures.name: Mirza Ghalib
figures.role: the greatest Urdu poet; his work became the canon of Urdu literature

sources: Ralph Russell, Ghalib: Life and Letters
outcome: Established the gold standard for Urdu poetry that continues to define the language's literary tradition

title: All India Muslim League founded
description: Muslim leaders in British India found the All India Muslim League in Dhaka to protect Muslim political interests, eventually becoming the vehicle for the creation of Pakistan.
outcome: Created the political party that would demand and achieve a separate Muslim state in South Asia

title: Young Turk Revolution restores Ottoman constitution
description: The Young Turks (Committee of Union and Progress) force Sultan Abdulhamid II to restore the 1876 constitution, beginning the final phase of Ottoman modernisation that will end with the empire's dissolution.

figures.name: Namik Kemal
figures.role: intellectual forefather of the Young Turk movement (died 1888)

sources: Erik Jan Zurcher, The Unionist Factor
outcome: Inaugurated the constitutional era that led to the abolition of the Ottoman sultanate and caliphate

title: Mustafa Kemal abolishes the Ottoman Caliphate
description: The Turkish Republic under Mustafa Kemal Ataturk formally abolishes the Ottoman Caliphate, ending 1,292 years of continuous caliphal authority in Sunni Islam and sending shockwaves through the Muslim world.
sources: Erik Jan Zurcher, Turkey: A Modern History
outcome: Ended the last institutional symbol of Muslim political unity and triggered a global debate about Islamic governance

title: Hasan al-Banna founds the Muslim Brotherhood
description: Egyptian schoolteacher Hasan al-Banna founds al-Ikhwan al-Muslimun (the Muslim Brotherhood) in Ismailia, Egypt, creating the most influential Islamist political organisation of the 20th century.

figures.name: Hasan al-Banna
figures.role: founder of the Muslim Brotherhood; created the template for Islamic political activism

sources: Richard Mitchell, The Society of the Muslim Brothers
outcome: Created the model for Islamic political activism that would inspire movements across the Muslim world

title: Allama Iqbal delivers the Allahabad Address
description: The poet-philosopher Allama Iqbal delivers his famous presidential address to the All India Muslim League at Allahabad, articulating the vision of a separate Muslim state in northwest India — the intellectual foundation of Pakistan.

figures.name: Allama Iqbal
figures.role: poet-philosopher; articulated the vision for Pakistan

sources: Iqbal, The Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam
outcome: Provided the intellectual and philosophical basis for the creation of Pakistan 17 years later

title: Kingdom of Saudi Arabia founded
description: Abdulaziz ibn Abdul Rahman Al Saud unifies the Hejaz and Nejd into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, creating a state built on the Wahhabi-Saudi alliance forged in 1744.
sources: Alexei Vassiliev, The History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: Created the modern Saudi state that would become the custodian of Islam's two holiest sites and the world's largest oil exporter

title: Bediuzzaman Said Nursi completes the Risale-i Nur
description: Turkish scholar Bediuzzaman Said Nursi completes the Risale-i Nur collection in exile and prison, a 6,000-page commentary on Quranic themes that argues for Islamic faith using rational and scientific reasoning.

figures.name: Bediuzzaman Said Nursi
figures.role: Turkish scholar; authored the Risale-i Nur collection in prison

sources: Sukran Vahide, Islam in Modern Turkey: An Intellectual Biography of Bediuzzaman Said Nursi
outcome: Created the most widely-read Islamic theological work of the 20th century, with millions of followers in Turkey and beyond

title: Pakistan created as the world's first Islamic republic
description: The partition of British India creates Pakistan — the world's first modern state founded on Muslim identity — from the Muslim-majority regions of the subcontinent. Millions are displaced and hundreds of thousands killed in sectarian violence.

figures.name: Allama Iqbal
figures.role: intellectual father of Pakistan (died 1938); his vision made real

outcome: Created the largest Muslim-majority nation at independence, fundamentally reshaping South Asian and Islamic geopolitics

title: First Arab-Israeli War and Palestinian displacement
description: Following the creation of Israel, five Arab armies attack but are defeated. Over 700,000 Palestinians are displaced in what they call the Nakba (catastrophe), creating a refugee crisis that persists to this day.
outcome: Created the Palestinian refugee crisis and the Arab-Israeli conflict that has defined Middle Eastern politics ever since

title: Egyptian Revolution — Nasser era begins
description: A military coup led by the Free Officers movement overthrows King Farouk of Egypt. Gamal Abdel Nasser emerges as leader, championing Arab nationalism, pan-Arabism and non-alignment.
outcome: Inaugurated the era of Arab nationalism and made Egypt the political centre of the Arab world

title: Algeria wins independence after 132 years of French rule
description: After a brutal eight-year war of independence that killed over a million Algerians, France recognises Algerian independence, ending 132 years of colonial rule.
sources: Alistair Horne, A Savage War of Peace
outcome: Became the most celebrated anti-colonial victory in the Muslim world and a symbol of Third World liberation

title: Malcolm X assassinated after Hajj transformation
description: Malcolm X (El-Hajj Malik el-Shabazz) is assassinated in New York. His 1964 pilgrimage to Mecca had transformed his worldview from racial separatism to universal Islamic brotherhood, documented in his famous letter from Mecca.

figures.name: Malcolm X
figures.role: American Muslim leader; assassinated after his spiritual transformation through Hajj

sources: Malcolm X and Alex Haley, The Autobiography of Malcolm X
outcome: His life and death became the most powerful story of Islamic spiritual transformation in the modern West

title: Sayyid Qutb executed — Islamist thought radicalises
description: Egyptian thinker Sayyid Qutb is executed by the Nasser regime. His prison writings, especially Milestones (Ma'alim fi al-Tariq), will become the foundational texts of radical Islamist ideology.

figures.name: Sayyid Qutb
figures.role: Muslim Brotherhood thinker; executed by the Egyptian state

outcome: His martyrdom and writings became the ideological foundation for radical Islamist movements globally

title: Six-Day War — Israel captures Jerusalem
description: Israel defeats Egypt, Syria and Jordan in six days, capturing the Sinai Peninsula, Gaza, the West Bank, the Golan Heights, and — most significantly for the Muslim world — East Jerusalem including al-Aqsa Mosque and the Dome of the Rock.
sources: Michael Oren, Six Days of War
outcome: The loss of Jerusalem galvanised Islamic activism worldwide and discredited secular Arab nationalism

title: Annemarie Schimmel publishes Mystical Dimensions of Islam
description: The German scholar Annemarie Schimmel publishes Mystical Dimensions of Islam, the most comprehensive and sympathetic Western scholarly introduction to Sufism, making Islamic mysticism accessible to a global audience.

figures.name: Annemarie Schimmel
figures.role: German scholar; authored the definitive English introduction to Sufism

sources: Annemarie Schimmel, Mystical Dimensions of Islam
outcome: Made Sufism accessible to Western readers and established it as a legitimate field of academic study

title: Iranian Revolution — Ayatollah Khomeini founds Islamic Republic
description: The Shah of Iran is overthrown in a popular revolution led by Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, who establishes the Islamic Republic of Iran — the first modern theocratic state based on the concept of wilayat al-faqih (guardianship of the jurist).

figures.name: Ayatollah Khomeini
figures.role: leader of the revolution; established the Islamic Republic of Iran

sources: Ervand Abrahamian, Iran Between Two Revolutions
outcome: Created the first modern Islamic state, inspired Islamist movements worldwide and reshaped Middle Eastern geopolitics

title: Soviet Union invades Afghanistan — global jihad begins
description: The Soviet Union invades Afghanistan to support the communist government, triggering a decade-long jihad by Afghan mujahideen backed by the USA, Saudi Arabia and Pakistan.
outcome: Created the global jihad movement, led to the rise of al-Qaeda and reshaped Muslim geopolitics for decades

title: Seizure of the Grand Mosque in Mecca
description: On the first day of the 15th Islamic century (1 Muharram 1400), armed militants seize the Grand Mosque in Mecca, declaring their leader as the Mahdi. Saudi forces retake the mosque after two weeks of fighting.
sources: Yaroslav Trofimov, The Siege of Mecca
outcome: Shocked the Muslim world and led Saudi Arabia to dramatically increase religious conservatism and global Islamic funding

title: Soviet withdrawal from Afghanistan — mujahideen victorious
description: The Soviet Union completes its withdrawal from Afghanistan after a decade-long war that killed over a million Afghans. The mujahideen victory is seen across the Muslim world as a triumph of faith over a superpower.
outcome: Emboldened the global jihad movement and produced a generation of fighters who dispersed across the Muslim world

title: September 11 attacks and US invasion of Afghanistan
description: Al-Qaeda operatives hijack four commercial airliners and attack the World Trade Center and Pentagon, killing nearly 3,000 people. The United States invades Afghanistan to destroy al-Qaeda and remove the Taliban government.
outcome: Triggered the global 'War on Terror' that reshaped the Muslim world and Muslim life in the West

title: US-led invasion of Iraq
description: A US-led coalition invades Iraq and topples Saddam Hussein, triggering sectarian civil war, the destruction of Iraqi civil society and the eventual rise of ISIS.
sources: Patrick Cockburn, The Rise of Islamic State
outcome: Destabilised Iraq and the wider Middle East, fuelling sectarian conflict and extremist movements

title: Amman Message unites Muslim scholars on interfaith respect
description: 200 of the world's leading Muslim scholars from 50 countries issue the Amman Message, formally recognising eight schools of Islamic jurisprudence and declaring takfir (excommunication) against fellow Muslims impermissible.
outcome: Produced the broadest consensus statement by Muslim scholars in modern history, repudiating extremist theology

title: Arab Spring begins in Tunisia
description: The self-immolation of Mohamed Bouazizi in Tunisia sparks mass protests that topple President Ben Ali, triggering uprisings across the Arab world from Egypt to Syria, Libya, Yemen and Bahrain.
outcome: Triggered the most significant political upheaval in the Arab world since decolonisation

title: Egyptian Revolution topples Mubarak
description: Eighteen days of mass protests in Tahrir Square force President Hosni Mubarak to resign after 30 years in power, briefly raising hopes for democratic governance across the Muslim world.
outcome: Briefly raised hopes for Arab democracy before a military coup in 2013 restored authoritarian rule

title: ISIS declares a 'caliphate' in Iraq and Syria
description: The Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant (ISIS) captures Mosul and declares a 'worldwide caliphate' under Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi. Muslim scholars worldwide condemn the declaration as illegitimate.
sources: Patrick Cockburn, The Rise of Islamic State
outcome: Provoked a global military response and a unified scholarly rejection of extremist theology

title: Muslim scholars issue Open Letter to ISIS
description: Over 120 leading Muslim scholars from around the world issue a detailed Open Letter to al-Baghdadi refuting ISIS ideology point by point using Islamic jurisprudence, the most comprehensive scholarly refutation of jihadist extremism.
outcome: Produced the most authoritative scholarly refutation of extremist ideology in Islamic history

title: Document on Human Fraternity signed in Abu Dhabi
description: The Grand Imam of Al-Azhar, Ahmad al-Tayyeb, and Pope Francis sign the Document on Human Fraternity for World Peace in Abu Dhabi, the most significant interfaith declaration between Christianity and Islam.
outcome: Established a new framework for Muslim-Christian dialogue endorsed at the highest levels of both faiths

title: Taliban retake Afghanistan as US withdraws
description: The Taliban seize control of Afghanistan in August as the United States completes its military withdrawal after 20 years of war, capturing Kabul in days as the Afghan government collapses.
sources: Carter Malkasian, The American War in Afghanistan
outcome: Ended the longest war in American history and raised urgent questions about Islamic governance and women's rights

title: October 7 attack and Gaza war
description: Hamas launches a major attack on southern Israel on October 7, killing approximately 1,200 people. Israel responds with a devastating military campaign in Gaza that kills tens of thousands of Palestinians and triggers a global humanitarian crisis.
outcome: Reignited the Palestinian question as the central issue of Muslim geopolitics and provoked a worldwide debate about international law

title: Fall of the Assad regime in Syria
description: In December 2024, opposition forces launch a major offensive that topples the Assad regime after 13 years of civil war, ending over 50 years of Assad family rule in Syria.
outcome: Ended decades of authoritarian rule and opened an uncertain new chapter for Syria and the wider region
